Bruenig acknowledges that his scheme isn't the only way to correct wealth inequality, but argues that 'it is one of the few ways that we know works well and is able to work within the system we now have.'}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last=Franklin |first=Peter |date=January 18, 2018 |title=If governments do raise taxes on wealth, what should be done with the revenues? |url=https://unherd.com/2018/01/governments-raise-taxes-wealth-done-revenues/ |access-date=January 10, 2025 |website=UnHerd |quote=Are the rich getting richer? They certainly are in America, according to Matt Bruenig in the ''New York Times'': 'The wealth of the top 1 percent increased by an average of $4.9 million over the past decade, while the average holdings of the bottom 99 percent declined by about $4,500. Wealth inequality is now the highest it has been since the Federal Reserve began collecting this kind of data in 1983.' <!-- Breunig [''sic''] -->[Bruenig], however, has a solution – a sort of sovereign wealth fund that he calls a 'social wealth fund': 'Here's how it could work. The federal government would create and run a new investment fund, and issue every adult citizen one share of ownership. The fund would gradually come to own a substantial and diverse portfolio of stocks, bonds and real estate. The investment return that the fund generates would be paid out to each citizen in the form of a universal basic dividend, and the shares would be nontransferable to preserve the institution’s egalitarian purpose.' ... 'If, over time, the social wealth fund came to own one-third of the country’s wealth, that would allow it to distribute an annual dividend equivalent to about a third of the total returns on invested capital each year, which represents about a tenth of net national income. In 2016, based on the latest available census population figures, that would have meant around $6,400 paid to all adults or $8,000 paid to every person between the ages of 18 and 64.' ... Breunig notes that the 'key challenge in building a social wealth fund is not how to run it once it has been created, but how to bring assets into the fund in the first place.' ... His ideas for capitalising the fund include the following: '...the transfer of existing federal assets like land, buildings and portions of the wireless spectrum into the new fund... increases in taxes on capital that affect mostly the wealthy such as estate, dividend and financial transaction taxes and the creation of a new type of corporate tax that requires companies to directly issue new shares to the social wealth fund on an annual basis and during certain corporate moves such as initial public offerings, mergers and acquisitions.'}}</ref> as one way to reduce [[income inequality in the United States]],<ref>{{cite news |last=Bruenig |first=Matt |date=November 30, 2017 |title=A Simple Fix for Our Massive Inequality Problem |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2017/11/30/opinion/inequality-social-wealth-fund.html |access-date=January 9, 2025 |work=The New York Times |issn=1553-8095}}</ref><ref name="Yglesias 2017">{{cite web |last=Yglesias |first=Matthew |date=December 14, 2017 |title=Collective ownership of the means of production |url=https://www.vox.com/policy-and-politics/2017/12/14/16778020/collective-ownership-means-of-production |access-date=January 10, 2025 |website=Vox}}</ref> with Noah Smith writing that it "seems extremely promising and woefully overlooked".<ref>{{cite news|last=Smith |first=Noah |date=December 5, 2017 |title=Robot Takeover Matters Less If We're All Shareholders |url=https://www.bloomberg.com/opinion/articles/2017-12-05/robot-takeover-matters-less-if-we-re-all-shareholders |access-date=January 9, 2025 |work=Bloomberg News}}</ref> Smith suggested that Bruenig's plan would not only fix inequality but also find "a way to insure the American middle and working class against technological change".<ref>{{cite web |last=Dumitriu |first=Sam |date=December 6, 2024 |title=Where Paul Ryan and Matt Bruenig agree |url=https://www.adamsmith.org/blog/where-paul-ryan-and-matt-bruenig-agree |access-date=January 9, 2025 |publisher=Adam Smith Institute}}</ref> In ''[[Democracy (journal)|Democracy]]'',<!-- https://democracyjournal.org/magazine/12/seeking-swf/ --> sociologist [[Dalton Conley]] also called, in 2009 and due to the [[2008 financial crisis]], for the establishment of an American sovereign wealth fund.<ref name="Yglesias 2017"/> In addition to his concept of social wealth funds, Bruenig published a paper<!-- http://peoplespolicyproject.org/wp-content/uploads/2018/04/SocialHousing.pdf --> authored by ''The Week'' writer Ryan Cooper and Dublin-based researcher Saoirse Gowan arguing that the best response to the issue of housing affordability would be a massive social housing project, in which the government would pay to build ten million homes over ten years, pointing to the success of such a program in European countries like Austria and Sweden.<ref name="Edelman 2018"/>

=== Universal basic income ===
Bruenig writes that an [[universal basic income]] is "a way of dealing with the capital problem" but that for the "[[Silicon Valley]] types and the libertarian types" is "a remedy to the problems their innovation is causing or that they think their innovation will cause", and describes the possible implementation by them as a "terrible ... very dystopian idea".<ref name="Denvir 2017"/> Bruenig also says that passive income already exists in capitalist societies. He writes: "[C]apitalist societies already dedicate a large portion of their economic outputs to paying out money to people who have not worked for it. The UBI does not invent passive income. It merely doles it out evenly to everyone in society, rather than in very concentrated amounts to the richest people in society."<ref>{{cite web|last=McFarland|first=Kate|date=February 26, 2017|title=Matt Bruenig on 'Passive Income': Viral Article and Video Interview|url=http://basicincome.org/news/2017/02/matt-bruenig-viral-article-universal-basic-income-interview-video/|publisher=Basic Income Earth Network|access-date=January 9, 2025}}</ref> Bruenig thus argues in favor of a basic income for all,<ref>{{cite magazine |last=Worstall |first=Tim <!-- |author-link=Tim Worstall --> |date=January 4, 2017 |title=Matt Bruenig Says the 1% Already Gets a Universal Basic Income – So Why Not One for All? |url=https://www.forbes.com/sites/timworstall/2017/01/04/matt-bruenig-says-the-1-already-gets-a-universal-basic-income-so-why-not-one-for-all |access-date=January 9, 2025 |magazine=Forbes |issn=0015-6914}}</ref> and provides a list of taxation possibilities for creating a Social Wealth Fund capable of paying an universal income.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Stafford |first=Richard Todd |date=2019 |title=Response to Caroline West's 'From Company Town to Post-Industrial: Inquiry on the Redistribution of Space and Capital with a Universal Basic Income' |journal=Lateral |volume=8 |issue=1 |doi=10.25158/L8.1.11 |issn=2469-4053 |jstor=48671446 |doi-access=free }}</ref> He further states, in response to [[Amazon (company)|Amazon]]'s raise of wages at least [[$15 per hour]], that "[w]age levels are determined as much by social forces as they are by market forces", and that "wage-setting is driven not by [[invisible hand]]s, but by the decisions of real people who can be affected by collective-pressure tactics and other forms of social power".<ref>{{cite news |last=Bruenig |first=Matt |date=October 4, 2018 |title=Democrats, take note: If you want to raise wages, put pressure on employers |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/opinions/democrats-take-note-if-you-want-to-raise-wages-put-pressure-on-employers/2018/10/04/486b0620-c756-11e8-b2b5-79270f9cce17_story.html |access-date=January 9, 2025 |newspaper=The Washington Post |issn=0190-8286}}</ref> He argues that simply increasing [[unemployment benefits]] rather than using [[Stimulus (economics)|stimulus]] checks would be insufficient in helping the poorest Americans.<ref>{{cite magazine |last=Page |first=Amanda |date=March 9, 2021 |title=Why Passing Stimulus Checks is Critical to the Democratic Party's Future |url=https://www.proquest.com/openview/a6f561eb0e33c7ecead3b9937cc686f6/1?pq-origsite=gscholar&cbl=7030806 |access-date=January 9, 2025 |magazine=Brown Political Review}}</ref>

=== Healthcare, education, and other policies ===
Bruenig is an advocate of [[single-payer healthcare]],<ref name="Scott 2019">{{cite web|last=Scott|first=Dylan|date=March 4, 2019|title=A single-payer advocate answers the big question: How do we pay for it?|url=https://www.vox.com/policy-and-politics/2019/3/4/18249888/medicare-for-all-cost-matt-bruenig-voxcare|access-date=January 9, 2025|website=Vox}}</ref> and has argued extensively in favor of its feasibility.<ref>{{cite magazine|first=Matt|last=Bruenig|title=Don't Believe the Hype: Paying for Medicare for All Is Simple|url=https://jacobinmag.com/2019/03/medicare-for-all-government-spending-taxes|access-date=January 9, 2025|magazine=Jacobin|issn=2158-2602}}</ref> He says that "[[Medicaid]] was the most effective and most popular part of [[Obamacare]]" and that it "kept the [Obamacare] afloat", showing that "this simple public-health insurance program ends up creating far better constituencies and support bases than these complicated [[Rube Goldberg]] machines like Obamacare".<ref name="Denvir 2017"/> He wrote an article making the case that liberal critics of single-payer healthcare are "moral monsters" on par with proponents of [[Trumpcare]].<ref name="Denvir 2017"/> Bruenig later explained: "Moving from Obamacare to Trumpcare, twenty-four million people would lose their insurance. By the same token, keeping Obamacare instead of single-payer will keep twenty-eight million people from being insured. They're similar magnitudes. In fact, the Obamacare to single-payer magnitude is higher."<ref name="Denvir 2017"/> As an advocate [[Medicare for All]],<ref>{{cite magazine |last=Gaffney |first=Adam |date=2018 |title=The Two Souls of U.S. Healthcare |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/pub/56/article/692138/summary |magazine=Dissent |volume=65 |issue=2 |pages=59–68 |issn=1946-0910 |access-date=January 9, 2025}}</ref> he argues that it would cut poverty by over 20 percent, lifting 8 million people out of poverty and at the same time generating economic growth as individuals invest more into the economy by spending on consumer goods.<ref name="Scott 2019"/><ref>{{cite web |last=Bruenig |first=Matt |date=September 12, 2019 |title=Medicare for All Would Cut Poverty by Over 20 Percent |url=https://www.peoplespolicyproject.org/2019/09/12/medicare-for-all-would-cut-poverty-by-over-20-percent/ |access-date=January 9, 2025 |publisher=People's Policy Project}}</ref>

Bruenig was skeptical of free higher education, later commenting: "My issue was not Clinton's issue, which is that it supports rich students when it should support poor students. My position is that providing benefits to students ignores non-students."<ref name="Denvir 2017" /> He supports the education system in Finland and Sweden, where "benefits paid to students are paid out from the same welfare agency that you would go to if you were a single mother and didn't have a job".<ref name="Denvir 2017" /> As a solution to the  problem of two-tier system of disability benefits, Bruenig suggests guaranteeing a minimum [[Social Security Disability Insurance]] benefit to all people with disabilities that is equal to the federal poverty line regardless of work history, earnings, or assets.<ref>{{cite magazine |last=Saxena |first=Akhil |date=April 7, 2022 |title=Ending the Two-Tier System of Disability Benefits |url=https://www.proquest.com/openview/6fd47a55a20f4177a79969384a93ab9c/1?pq-origsite=gscholar&cbl=7030806 |access-date=January 9, 2025 |magazine=Brown Political Review |language=}}</ref> He also wrote an article about school reform,<ref>{{cite web|last=Campos|first=Paul<!-- |author-link=Paul Campos -->|date=April 23, 2014|title=Matt Bruenig Discovers Secret Motivation for Criticisms of His Insights|url=http://www.lawyersgunsmoneyblog.com/2014/04/matt-bruenig-discovers-secret-motivation-criticisms-insights|website=Lawyers Guns & Money|access-date=January 9, 2025}}</ref> arguing that "we should do what tons of other countries do and make it easier to be a lawyer" rather than "creating massive barriers to entering the job of reading and writing arguments and following made up procedures".<!-- https://mattbruenig.com/2014/04/22/law-professor-argues-protectionism-for-law-professors-definitely-good/ --><ref>{{cite magazine |last=Bruenig |first=Matt |date=April 21, 2014 |title=The case for killing law school |url=https://theweek.com/articles/447736/case-killing-law-school |access-date=January 9, 2025 |magazine=The Week |issn=1533-8304}}</ref>

Although commitment to an universal welfare state and [[competition policy]] are not mutually exclusive, Bruenig has been a critic of the [[Anti-monopoly (competition law)|anti-monopoly]] movement, such as the anti-trust [[New Brandeis movement]]. Critics like Bruenig and Matthew Yglesias<!-- https://www.slowboring.com/p/small-business-is-not-the-answer https://www.slowboring.com/p/what-market-does-amazon-monopolize https://mattbruenig.com/2021/06/03/the-anti-bigness-ideology/ https://www.spokesman.com/stories/2024/dec/19/matthew-yglesias-too-many-democrats-still-dont-get/ --> refer to it as the anti-bigness movement.<ref>{{cite magazine |last1=Bruenig |first1=Matt |last2=Teachout |first2=Zephyr |date=September 27, 2014 |title=Should the Government Break Up Big Corporations or Buy Them? |url=https://www.thenation.com/article/economy/nationalize-corporations-anti-monopoly-debate/ |access-date=January 10, 2025 |magazine=The Nation |issn=0027-8378 |quote=In recent years, there has been a resurgent anti-bigness movement on the American left, with more and more people claiming that the central problem with our economy is not that it is too capitalistic, that it lacks unions, or that it offers too little security in the form of the welfare state. Rather, according to anti-bigness campaigners, the economic problem of our time is that production is spread out across too few firms. ... Most anti-bigness advocates will not find this approach satisfactory, in part because they have certain goals that the public ownership of large enterprises does not accomplish. For instance, the foundational texts of the modern anti-bigness movement often argue that we should consider the ability to successfully operate a small business to be an important tentpole of individual liberty. But if you don't subscribe to some of these more boutique elements of anti-bigness and are mostly concerned about market power, selective public ownership does everything that breaking up big companies does, except better and faster.}}</ref> In 2018, Bruenig told Gilad Edelman that competition policy was for him "way down on the list of priorities".<ref name="Edelman 2018"/> According to Edelman, among the American socialist left, with the exception of Ryan Cooper, "there seems to be a reluctance among the socialist left to engage with an agenda that promotes competition. What is the socialist answer to the dominance of Amazon, Facebook, and Google?"<ref name="Edelman 2018"/> In a 2024 debate on ''[[The Nation]]'', Bruenig argued that the government should buy the big corporations, citing the example of the [[Tennessee Valley Authority]], contra [[Zephyr Teachout]] arguing that they should be broken up rather than [[nationalized]].<ref>{{cite magazine |last1=Bruenig |first1=Matt |last2=Teachout |first2=Zephyr |date=September 27, 2014 |title=Should the Government Break Up Big Corporations or Buy Them? |url=https://www.thenation.com/article/economy/nationalize-corporations-anti-monopoly-debate/ |access-date=January 10, 2025 |magazine=The Nation |issn=0027-8378 |quote=I don't think this is the correct diagnosis. I believe that rather than attempt to indirectly alter a company's behavior by trying to construct some kind of perfectly balanced market of private competitors, the government should, in most cases, just buy the company, keep its productive capacity intact, and then use its ownership rights to change its behaviors. ... A much better approach would be to simply purchase one of the big four meatpacking companies and run it as a federally owned enterprise like the TVA. The publicly traded Tyson Foods has a market capitalization of less than $25 billion. For that relatively small sum, which would not be lost to the federal government but rather invested in Tyson stock, the government could instantly direct one of the biggest meatpacking companies to stop using its market power to squeeze ranchers and customers. This, in turn, would force the other meat-packers to do the same or risk losing share to the now federally owned Tyson Foods.}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=September 27, 2024 |title=The Nation: Prof. Zephyr Teachout Argues that Freedom Requires Decentralized Power |url=https://news.law.fordham.edu/blog/2024/09/27/the-nation-prof-zephyr-teachout-nationalize-corporations-anti-monopoly-debate/ |access-date=January 10, 2025 |website=Fordham Law News}}</ref>

== Personal life ==
Since 2014, Bruenig has been married to Elizabeth Bruenig ({{nee|Stoker}}), a staff writer for ''The Atlantic''<!-- <ref>{{cite magazine |url=https://www.theatlantic.com/press-releases/archive/2021/05/elizabeth-bruenig-joins-atlantic-staff-writer/618869/ |title= Elizabeth Bruenig to Join The Atlantic as a Staff Writer |last=Benet |first=James |magazine=The Atlantic |date=May 12, 2021 |access-date=January 9, 2025|issn=2151-9463}}</ref> --> and formerly an opinion writer and editor at ''The Washington Post'' and ''The New York Times'',<!-- <ref>{{cite news|last1=Hiatt|first1=Fred|author1-link=Fred Hiatt|last2=Marcus|first2=Ruth|author2-link=Ruth Marcus (journalist)|last3=Diehl|first3=Jackson|author3-link=Jackson Diehl|date=October 25, 2017|title=Elizabeth Bruenig Joins Opinions Staff as Writer and Editor|url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/pr/wp/2017/10/25/elizabeth-bruenig-joins-opinions-staff-as-writer-and-editor/|department=WashPost PR Blog|work=The Washington Post|access-date=January 9, 2025}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.nytco.com/press/elizabeth-bruenig-joins-opinion/ |title=Elizabeth Bruenig Joins Opinion |last=Benet |first=James |work=The New York Times |quote=Elizabeth Bruenig will join The New York Times as an Opinion writer. |date=December 23, 2019 |access-date=January 9, 2025}}</ref> --> whom he met in their high school debate team in Arlington.<ref name="Edelman 2018"/> They have two children,<!-- <ref>{{cite news |last=Collins |first=Lois M. |title=Meet the socialist Catholic who confounds the left and the right |url=https://www.deseret.com/2022/4/25/23025656/the-contrarian-liz-bruenig-progressive-catholic-atlantic-theology |work=Deseret News |issn=0745-4724 |access-date=January 9, 2025 |date=April 26, 2022}}</ref> --><ref name="Yglesias 2020"/> and also host together ''The Bruenigs Podcast''. In 2020, [[Bloomberg News]] reported that Bruenig was producing a podcast, alongside his wife, that generated about $9,000 per month from listeners.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.bnnbloomberg.ca/left-wing-podcasters-are-charting-a-future-without-bernie-sanders-1.1441051 |title=Left-Wing Podcasters Are Charting A Future Without Bernie Sanders |last= Newcomer |first=Eric |work=Bloomberg News |quote='Left leaning podcasts have been listener funded,' says Matt Bruenig who hosts a podcast with his wife. ... 'With the podcast there are no costs so it's up to basically $100,000 a year. That's pretty good money.'|date=May 26, 2020 |access-date=January 9, 2025}}</ref>

Matt Bruenig was diagnosed as [[autistic]] in adulthood.<!-- <ref>{{cite podcast|url=https://soundcloud.com/ebruenig/medicare-for-all-fact-checking-debacle-and-autism|title=The Bruenigs: Medicare for All Fact-checking Debacle and Autism|website=Soundcloud|publisher=The Bruenigs|host=Bruenig, Elizabeth; Bruenig, Matt|date=August 2018|time=34:14|access-date=January 9, 2025}}</ref> --><ref>{{cite magazine |last=Goodman |first=Lawrence |date=2021 |title=The Examined Life |url=https://www.brandeis.edu/magazine/2021/fall/featured-stories/bruenig.html |access-date=January 9, 2025 |magazine=Brandeis Magazine |issue=Fall 2021}}</ref>
